questions.question: An Ir-192 source was certified at 120 Ci exactly 74 days ago. Using 0.5 R/h per curie at 1 m, the dose rate at 10 m from the open source today is about:
options: 3 R/h
300 mR/h
600 mR/h
150 mR/h
questions.explanation: 74 days is one half-life, so 120 Ci has decayed to 60 Ci, giving 30 R/h at 1 m; the inverse square law over 10 m divides by 10² = 100, leaving 0.3 R/h = 300 mR/h.

questions.question: Which single change produces the largest reduction in the dose rate you stand in at the crank position?
options: Doubling your distance from the exposure point
Adding one half-value layer of shielding
Halving the exposure clock time
Switching to a finer-grain film
questions.explanation: Doubling distance cuts the rate by 2² = 4, while one HVL only halves it; halving exposure time changes your dose, not the rate you stand in, and film choice changes neither.

questions.question: An open-beam survey put the 2 mR/h boundary at 100 ft. A collimator that cuts the rate by a factor of 10 in its shielded directions is installed. In those directions the new boundary should fall at roughly:
options: 10 ft, with no confirmation needed
32 ft, then confirmed with the survey meter
50 ft, then confirmed with the survey meter
100 ft - collimators do not move boundaries
questions.explanation: A factor of 10 in rate pulls the boundary in by √10 ≈ 3.2, so about 100 ÷ 3.2 ≈ 32 ft - and the meter still sets the final rope, because scatter distorts ideal geometry.

questions.question: A written ALARA program typically sets investigation levels well below the regulatory dose limits. Their purpose is to:
options: Define new legal limits for the crew
Trigger review and correction of unusual doses long before any limit is threatened
Replace the daily reading of pocket dosimeters
Establish the level at which boundaries are posted
questions.explanation: Investigation levels are tripwires set at fractions of the limits so that an odd reading gets investigated and the practice corrected while the dose involved is still small.

questions.question: Your pre-job budget for a 20-shot shift was 8 mR, but your assistant's pocket dosimeter shows 30 mR at shift end - far under every limit. The Level II response consistent with ALARA is to:
options: File the reading; no limit was approached
Investigate the same day, find the practice that drove the extra dose, correct it, and document the finding
Reassign the assistant permanently to darkroom work
Raise the next shift's budget to 30 mR so the plan matches reality
questions.explanation: A reading nearly four times the budget on identical work means a habit or layout problem - someone standing in the falling field, a short crank position. ALARA requires finding and fixing it, not normalizing it.

questions.question: Restricted-area boundaries for radiography are commonly posted near the 2 mR/h line because:
options: That is the highest rate a GM tube can read accurately
Typical regulations limit members of the public to about 2 mrem in any one hour and about 100 mrem per year
Survey meters cannot resolve rates below 2 mR/h
Two millirem per hour marks one half-value layer of air
questions.explanation: Keeping unbadged workers and the public behind a measured, watched ≈2 mR/h line supports the typical public dose limits - the boundary is placed by survey and defended by surveillance.

questions.question: After a retrieval failure, the source may be stranded somewhere along 20 ft of guide tube under scaffold debris. The professional instrument combination for finding it is:
options: A pocket dosimeter to localize it, with the badge as confirmation
An alarming ratemeter waved along the tube
An ion chamber governing the approach, with a scintillation instrument to localize the weak, shielded source
An uncompensated GM probe pressed against the tube
questions.explanation: The ion chamber stays linear in the intense field near the stranded source and keeps the searcher out of trouble, while the scintillator's sensitivity pinpoints a source that debris or shielding has muffled.

questions.question: The approach survey after every exposure is made with an ion chamber rather than a GM instrument chiefly because the ion chamber:
options: Is more sensitive to very low-level fields
Weighs less and responds faster
Remains accurate and linear at the very high rates a stranded source can produce, where a GM may saturate and read low
Never requires periodic calibration
questions.explanation: A saturated GM can display nearly zero in an intense field - the worst possible lie during an approach. The ion chamber reads truthfully from boundary levels up through tens of R/h.

questions.question: Device standards allow a loaded gamma projector to read up to roughly 200 mrem/h at its surface. The practical handling consequence is:
options: The device is safe to sit on between exposures
Handling stays brief and purposeful - about three minutes carried against the body can cost about 10 mrem
The device must be stored inside the posted exposure boundary
A surface reading proves the source is fully shielded
questions.explanation: At 200 mR/h, 3 minutes is 10 mR, so loaded devices are carried by the handle, handled briefly, and stored away from people - and only a survey of the device and the full retrieval train proves the source is home.

questions.question: Which safety-relevant operating characteristic separates X-ray machines from gamma exposure devices?
options: X-ray tubes need no warm-up after idle periods
A de-energized X-ray tube emits nothing, while a gamma source emits continuously and must be shielded, secured, and surveyed at all times
Gamma sources can be switched off with the device key
Duty-cycle limits apply mainly to gamma devices
questions.explanation: Kill the kilovoltage and X-radiation is gone; a gamma source is never off, which drives the storage, security, transport, and survey-after-every-exposure disciplines of source radiography.
